ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for generic wafer bar; SNOMED CT coverage of processed snack subcategories is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve wafer bar weighs approximately 35g, equivalent to one individually wrapped bar or two to three wafer fingers.

nutrient_source.title: Wafer bar (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Wafer bar (any flavour)', CR ID F11080076. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for a generic flavoured wafer bar was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry: plain sweet wafer biscuits (wheat flour, sugar, fat base), which are classified as high-GI foods (GI ~76) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 21.1) / 100 ≈ 16.1, using a 35g serving containing approximately 21.1g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (37.9g/100g) with low fibre (1.2g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat at ~14g/100g contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit wafer bars, as the combination of high sugar, high saturated fat, and moderate sodium (166mg/100g) collectively elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk; if consumed, restrict to no more than one small piece (~15g) on rare occasions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 37.9g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrates at 60.3g/100g with only 1.2g/100g dietary fibre produces an extremely high glycaemic load (GL ~16 per 35g serving), causing rapid postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wafer bars are contraindicated for Type 2 Diabetes management due to their very high sugar and refined carbohydrate content; if consumed as an occasional treat, limit to one small piece (~15g) and pair with a protein source to partially blunt the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~14g/100g from palm oil and trans fat at ~0.3g/100g both raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherogenesis; combined with high sugar driving triglyceride synthesis, this product presents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid wafer bars due to the co-occurrence of high saturated fat (~14g/100g), trans fat (~0.3g/100g), and high sugar (37.9g/100g), all of which independently and synergistically worsen lipid profiles and endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.3g/100g) triggers the RA hard gate; saturated fat at ~14g/100g from palm oil exceeds the 5g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; sugar at 37.9g/100g far exceeds the 15g/100g pro-inflammatory ceiling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid wafer bars entirely, as trans fat from partially hydrogenated vegetable fat is strongly pro-inflammatory at any amount, and the high saturated fat and sugar content further promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A wafer bar is an ultra-processed sweet snack consisting of multiple thin, crispy baked wafer layers sandwiched with a sweetened cream or flavoured filling, typically chocolate, vanilla, strawberry, or hazelnut. The wafer sheets are made from wheat flour, water, and leavening agents baked into a light, airy, brittle structure, while the filling is composed primarily of sugar, vegetable fat (commonly palm oil), and flavouring agents. The product is available in individually wrapped single-serve bars or multi-finger formats. Nutritionally, wafer bars are energy-dense at 517 kcal per 100g, with a high carbohydrate content of 60.3g/100g dominated by sugar at 37.9g/100g, fat at 27.5g/100g (predominantly saturated fat from palm oil at approximately 14g/100g and trace trans fat at ~0.3g/100g), and modest protein at 6.5g/100g. Dietary fibre is very low at 1.2g/100g. The glycaemic index is high (~76), and a standard 35g serving delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 16, making this product unsuitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, wafer bars are contraindicated for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to their high sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and low fibre profile. They are widely consumed as a convenience snack across Singapore and globally, often sold in supermarkets, convenience stores, and vending machines.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a wafer bar?
Is wafer bar high in sugar?
Can I eat wafer bars if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a wafer bar?
Is wafer bar suitable for heart patients?
How much fat is in a wafer bar?
What are the ingredients in a wafer bar?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080076), an approved primary source with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat estimates are derived from typical palm-oil-based wafer bar formulations consistent with USDA FoodData Central analogous entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet wafer biscuits.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is treated as a confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry (confirmed zero). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic wafer bar category are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F11080076 as of 2026. Saturated fat (~14g/100g) and trans fat (~0.3g/100g) are estimated from typical palm-oil-based wafer bar formulations and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Wafers, cream-filled' (FDC ID 168938); these values are not explicitly reported in the HPB source. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on plain sweet wafer biscuits from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. This food is a generic category record covering all flavour variants; individual branded products may differ in exact nutrient composition. Halal status is not confirmed for all variants; consumers should check individual product labelling.

improvement_actions_taken: Added saturated_fat_g (14.0) estimated from palm-oil-based wafer bar formulation cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central.
Added trans_fat_g (0.3) estimated from typical partially hydrogenated fat content in wafer bar cream filling.
Added GI of 76 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet wafer biscuits.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving (16.1) based on 35g serving and 60.34g/100g carbohydrate.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with defensible codes.
Expanded health_context to include all three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with clinical guidance.
Added Rheumatoid Arthritis as additional_condition preserving input health_suitability logic.
Expanded aliases to 8 English variants for retrieval coverage.
Documented all -1 sentinel values and confirmed zero for iron_mg in notes.
Set local_names all to null as this is a generic international product with no cuisine-specific mandatory language requirements.
